title: Engineering Services

labor_share.method: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $172,759.1M / sectoral output (T30) $367,702.1M, year 2024
sources: BLS Industry Productivity and Costs flat files (ip.data.1.AllData), measures L02 and T30

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $260,281 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541330); class <5: 27,901 firms, avg revenue/firm $412,809 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,605 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,267 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,523,903 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $238,491 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,702 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,089,183 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $483,457 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 4,840 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,897,160 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,548,906 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,297 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,835,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,703,745 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 685 firms, avg revenue/firm $252,830,867 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $39,568,031 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 6,137
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Engineer-of-record stamp, professional liability and field judgment are not automatable. Automatable sliver (~28%): calculation packages, code-compliance checks, report drafting, and preliminary modeling runs that AI can substantially draft. Remaining 72% is EOR oversight, client/agency interaction, complex judgment.
Schedule, budget, submittal tracking and RFI documentation are substantially automatable (~40%). Client and agency coordination, claims judgment, and relationship management persist.
Similar to civil: load calculations, energy modeling, specification drafting are AI-exposed (~25%). Responsible-charge oversight, equipment selection judgment, and field coordination persist.
Internal reporting, utilization tracking, and resource coordination automate (~15%). Client relationship management, business development, and strategic decision-making persist.

Production drawings and technical documentation are the most directly AI-automatable cost line (~75%). Judgment on constructability and client-specific standards persists at margin. Autodesk AutoCAD 2025/2026 AI features target this exact task set.
Load calculations, arc-flash studies, specification drafting are AI-exposed (~25%). EOR oversight, equipment coordination, utility coordination persist.
Internal administrative coordination, reporting, and scheduling automate (~15%). Strategic operations management, staffing decisions, and firm leadership persist.
AI-augmented coding assistance is well-documented (~30% of coding tasks). Concentrated in larger firms doing digital-delivery work; role augmented not eliminated.
Data reduction, drafting, and report preparation are substantially automatable (~55%). Physical lab and field testing has a physical-presence component that limits replacement.

Physical site presence for construction inspection is not automatable. AI/drone augmentation speeds some tasks but does not replace the in-person inspection requirement (~10% automatable for report writing and scheduling).

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted: Other engineering specialties (env/structural/geotech, ~12% of wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Environmental, structural, and geotechnical engineers have similar AI exposure to civil engineers (~25%): EOR stamp, site judgment, and regulatory interpretation persist; calculation packages, reports, and modeling automate.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted: Field/survey/inspection staff (~5% of wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical site presence for survey crews, field technicians, and as-built verification is not automatable. AI/drone augmentation helps but the physical presence requirement is the core product (~8% for report writing and data upload only).

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted: Other technical staff (senior engineers, non-top-10 PMs, ~11.95% of wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Senior engineers, project engineers, and additional PM roles not captured in top-10 OEWS have similar exposure to their listed counterparts (~25%). Professional judgment, stamped deliverables, and client interactions persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted: Admin/marketing/proposal staff (~18% of wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proposal production for QBS pursuits (a large overhead in A&E firms), scheduling, invoicing, document control, and marketing collateral are near-fully automatable (~65%). AI writing tools, CRM automation, and document management are deployed here now. Remaining 35% involves client relationship touches and firm strategy decisions.

pi_dist.rationale: Mixed billing environment. Public sector (~45% of revenue, based on ACEC/IBISWorld data: government is the dominant infrastructure client, state DOTs and federal agencies select via QBS and pay on cost-plus/FAR-audited overhead rates): when AI cuts direct labor hours, the audited overhead rate still applies to remaining labor but the total contract cost falls - savings pass to the public client, not the firm. Private/lump-sum sector (~55% of revenue): AI savings flow to firm margin on fixed-fee projects. However, competitive bidding in private markets will erode this advantage over the medium term. Composite weighted control: 0.45 * 0.20 (public, near-full pass-through) + 0.55 * 0.52 (private, partial retention amid competition) = 0.090 + 0.286 = 0.376, rounded to 0.38. On-call and framework agreement stickiness slightly supports the firm side.
sources: ACEC Research Institute 2024 Economic Assessment of Engineering + Design Services Industry (public/government sector dominance in infrastructure)
Monograph blog: Engineering Fees Pricing Models & Best Practices (fixed fee 59% of billing, T&M 23%, cost-plus for public clients at higher rate)
Cherry Bekaert: FAQs on Overhead Rate Audits & FAR Part 31 for A&E Firms (cost-plus/audited mechanism described)

pi_moat.rationale: Moderate barrier stack: (1) State PE licensure in every jurisdiction - engineering firm must employ a PE in responsible charge; client insourcing with AI alone is legally constrained. (2) Professional liability / E&O insurance requirements create credible commitment barriers. (3) On-call and framework agreements with public agencies are institutionally held by the firm (partially durable through acquisition). (4) Plan-stamp threat exists but responsible-charge rules require genuine PE oversight, not rubber-stamping - most state boards actively police this. (5) Limited proprietary data advantage; (6) No switching-cost lock-in in competitive QBS markets; (7) AI tooling equally available to large clients with internal PE staff (threat of insourcing growing). Net: licensure and liability moat is real but modest; competitive forces and AI-enabled insourcing erode it at the margins.
sources: ACEC: An Analysis of QBS in the Procurement of Engineering Services (QBS procurement mechanism described; no price competition at selection stage)
FAR Part 31 / AASHTO Uniform Audit Guide (overhead audit mechanism described)
State engineering licensure laws generally (PE responsible charge requirements in all 50 states)

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: Deltek Clarity 46th Annual Study (May 2025, ~700 US/Canada A&E firms): 53% of firms 'leveraging AI tools'. The 47th Annual Study (2026): 70% adoption with measurable positive impact only in 38% of respondents. Material adoption (AI actually displacing labor cost, not merely experimental) is estimated at ~38-40% of firms as of July 2026 (the 38% with measurable impact from the 47th study is the best proxy). Need to reach 50% material adoption. Historical analog: BIM adoption in AEC crossed 50% meaningful use around 2009-2010 (from near-zero in 2002-2004), taking ~7-8 years; by 2012 71% of all AEC firms used BIM meaningfully. AI adoption is accelerating faster than BIM did (year-over-year from 38% to 70% overall, 53% to 70% in one year). With 40% already at material level and acceleration, 50% is plausibly 2-3 years away. Use 3.0 years (mid of 2-3 year range).

current_adoption_pct.source: Deltek Clarity 46th Annual Architecture & Engineering Industry Study (2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 53% of A&E firms leveraging AI tools, up from 38% last year

historical_analogs.value: BIM adoption in AEC: ~7-10 years from ~10% to ~50% (approximately 2002-2010); CAD adoption in 1980s-1990s: ~10-12 years to majority. Both are slower than current AI trajectory.
historical_analogs.source: Multiple sources: BIM adoption research, McGraw Hill Construction SmartMarket Reports referenced in industry analyses
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: By 2012, 71% of architects, engineers, building contractors and building owners used BIM; 50% adoption reached approximately 2009-2010 starting from near-zero in 2002

owners_60plus_pct.source: Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report of AEC Firms; Consulting Specifying Engineer CEO/CFO demographics survey (Morrissey Goodale)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: The industry has an aging owner issue, with nearly 80 percent of firm owners looking to transition in the next decade. Average CEO age is 58 years old (Consulting Specifying Engineer/Morrissey Goodale data). Value/EBITDA ratio 4.23 in 2024.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report states 'the industry has an aging owner issue, with nearly 80 percent of firm owners looking to transition in the next decade.' Consulting Specifying Engineer/Morrissey Goodale data shows average CEO age of 58 and increasing CEO tenure (10 to 12 years), suggesting little C-suite transition occurring. Zweig 2025 Principals Report shows 82% prefer internal sale but internal pipeline is weak.

active_consolidators.source: Morrissey Goodale 2024 M&A Mid-Year Review; CTA Acquisitions / Auxo Capital advisors 2025 AEC M&A data; Westwood Professional Services 2025 M&A update
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed acquirers account for >50% of 500+ domestic transactions in 2025; number of PE-backed platforms in U.S. AEC sector has more than tripled since 2018. Named active PE-backed platforms: Ardurra (~$500M revenue), Verdantas (Sterling Group, ~$300M), IMEG (11 acquisitions in 2024), Salas O'Brien (7 acquisitions in 2024), Atwell (6 acquisitions), NV5 (7 US acquisitions), Legence/Blackstone, Bowman, ERG/Godspeed, Olsson/Morgan Stanley Capital Partners. Conservative count of 18 discrete PE-backed platforms active in last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: Zweig Group 2025 Valuation Report of AEC Firms (value/EBITDA 4.28 for median AEC firm); CTA Acquisitions 2026 engineering firm valuation guide (5x-7x for $1-3M EBITDA small firms)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Value/EBITDA ratio 4.28 (Zweig 2025 for median AEC firm with EBITDA ~$965K-$1.2M); 5x-7x EBITDA cited by CTA Acquisitions for single-office regional engineering firms ($1-3M EBITDA). Using 4.5x as mid-point reflecting EBITDA normalization: Zweig pre-bonus figure may overstate EBITDA vs. market-rate-comp basis; CTA figures likely post-normalization. EBITDA normalized per Rule 3 (market-rate owner compensation deducted).

exit_mult.source: CTA Acquisitions 2026 engineering firm valuation guide; Auxo Capital Advisors AEC valuation multiples guide
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Mid-size multi-state engineering firms ($8-25M EBITDA) command 8x-11x EBITDA. Using 8.5x as conservative lower bound for exit-size ($10M+ EBITDA) platforms. Premium-scale firms ($25M+) reach 10x-13x. EBITDA normalized per Rule 3.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed: ~45% of revenue under public/government cost-plus or QBS-negotiated hourly contracts with FAR/AASHTO audited overhead rates (DOT, federal, municipal clients); ~55% private sector fixed-fee/lump-sum or time-and-materials. Overall billing mix from Monograph/ACEC-BC data: fixed fee ~59%, T&M ~23%, cost-plus ~18% (though government T&M is underrepresented in these surveys). Public overhead-rate contracts require FAR Part 31 compliance; AI productivity gains on direct labor hours reduce total contract cost to client without flowing to firm margin.
pricing_structure.source: Monograph: Engineering Fees Pricing Models & Best Practices; Cherry Bekaert FAQs on FAR Part 31 overhead audits; ACEC-BC 2024 Fee Guideline
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Lump sum/fixed fee accounts for 59% of dominant pricing model; T&M 23%; government clients use T&M at nearly double rate of private (32% vs 19%). Public/DOT clients subject to FAR Part 31 overhead audit mechanism.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 15.62pp of revenue automatable. A = 3.33 (below 5). No tension: uplift well above 3pp and adoption speed is moderate (not fast enough to trigger the cross-check warning). Consistent: a large labor-cost wedge (33% of labor automatable) is available, but slow-ish adoption (t50=3 years) limits A score.

terminal_value.justification: Demand for licensed PE services on public infrastructure (roads, water, bridges, power) is structurally durable - regulatory and safety requirements mandate PE-stamped deliverables for the foreseeable future. However, AI-collapsed production hours in cost-plus/audited-overhead public contracts flow as savings to clients rather than to firm margin (FAR/AASHTO mechanism). For lump-sum private work, AI saves firm costs but competitive bidding will eventually pass savings to clients. The 5-10 year scenario: engineering remains a smaller-headcount, stamp-and-liability business with stable demand but compressed revenue per unit of output. AI cuts the operator's costs AND (in the public segment) also shrinks the billable base. Demand does not disappear but revenue/margin per project shrinks materially. Classification: PRESSURED.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from OEWS role mix analysis and task-level AI automation judgment (within_role fractions based on licensed vs. non-licensed task mix). A was derived from Deltek Clarity 46th/47th Annual Studies (AI adoption percentages) and BIM/CAD historical analog adoption curves. Neither V nor A referenced deal activity, PE platform counts, consolidation trends, or M&A multiples.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: ~45% of engineering revenue is under public/government QBS+audited-overhead contracts (FAR Part 31 / AASHTO mechanism). Under these contracts, AI efficiency on direct labor hours lowers the audited cost base - the firm cannot retain the savings because the fee is set as a multiple of audited actual cost. The remaining ~55% is private fixed-fee or T&M where savings accrue to the firm initially, but competitive bidding on future projects transfers savings to clients over time. This double-deflationary pressure is reflected in pi_dist=0.38: the firm controls less than half of the AI value it creates. pi_moat=0.45 reflects that PE licensure and liability requirements prevent complete client bypass but do not prevent the pricing pass-through mechanism.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=2.0: A=5.0, S rises to ~4.5. At t50=6.0: A=1.67, S falls to ~3.4. The t50 range moves S by roughly 0.5-0.6 points, the single largest uncertainty in this record.
Driven mainly by admin/proposal automation fraction (65%) and unlisted-category estimates. At 0.25: V_raw=0.1175, V=4.70, S falls ~0.3. At 0.42: V_raw=0.1973, V=7.89, S rises ~0.3.
Uncertainty in public vs private revenue split and degree of private-market competitive pass-through. At 0.25: C=1.125, S falls ~0.2. At 0.50: C=2.25, S rises ~0.3.

ESTIMATE; no direct primary data found. At 0.35: OW=clamp(0.714,0.1,0.9)=0.714+0.1=0.814 (with succession bonus), B=8.63, S ~4.08. At 0.55: OW already hits cap of 1.0 at all values above 0.45, so B and S unchanged once succession bonus applied.

reviewer_note: 1. EBITDA normalization: Zweig multiples are cited on 'pre-bonus profit' which may still include some above-market owner draws for principals working in the business. CTA Acquisitions figures appear to be on normalized EBITDA. buy_mult=4.5 is a judgment midpoint; actual post-normalization multiples for micro-cap (<$2M EBITDA) engineering firms may be as low as 3.5x. 2. Admin/proposal wage share: the unlisted ~18% for admin/marketing/proposal is an estimate derived by difference from listed OEWS occupations. If actual admin share is lower (e.g., 12%), ai_replaceable_share falls to ~0.29. 3. Public sector revenue share: no single authoritative source found for the 45% public estimate; it is consistent with IBISWorld general commentary on government as dominant buyer but could range 35-55%. 4. active_consolidators=18: conservative count of PE-backed platforms; Morrissey Goodale data implies the number could be 25-40+ given >500 deals/year with PE at >50% and many serial acquirers. Higher count raises CFD, lowers B, but effect is small. 5. The 2026 Deltek 47th Study shows 70% adoption overall but only 38% measurable impact -- this is the right denominator for material adoption; using 38-40% current material adoption as the t50 baseline.
